question: [File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: restore(long)] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a scenario where restore() is called with gas=0 (scaleGas(0) returns 0, so addTokens is skipped), but the rateLimitBucket token consumed in throttle() is never restored, causing permanent rate-token leakage during a partition where many zero-gas requests are processed, eventually depleting the rateLimitBucket and denying service?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user, during a network partition that causes load balancer failover to a single node, exploit the fact that the single node's rateLimitBucket is sized for the full cluster's requestsPerSecond (not per-node), causing the single node to accept the full cluster rate and become overwhelmed with requests it cannot process within design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the RandomUtils.secure().randomLong(0L, 100L) >= rate check in RequestProperties.test() — which only applies when action != THROTTLE — to statistically bypass LOG or REJECT filters at a rate of (100-rate)% of requests, and during a network partition where this randomness is independent per node, cause inconsistent enforcement across the cluster?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user, during a network partition, exploit the fact that the gasLimitBucket uses SynchronizationStrategy.SYNCHRONIZED (a JVM-level lock) while rateLimitBucket uses the default (lock-free) strategy, causing a thread contention imbalance under high load where the synchronized gasLimitBucket becomes a bottleneck, allowing the unsynchronized rateLimitBucket to over-consume tokens before the gas check can reject requests?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: restore(long)] Can an unprivileged external user trigger the ContractController's catch block for InvalidParametersException (which calls throttleManager.restore(request.getGas())) by submitting a request that passes throttle() but fails validateContractMaxGasLimit(), causing gas tokens to be restored even though the request was throttled, effectively making the gas throttle a no-op for requests that fail parameter validation?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that during a network partition, each node's bucket refill timer runs independently (based on local JVM clock), and if a node's clock drifts during partition, its bucket refills at a different rate than intended, causing the node to allow more or fewer requests than the design parameters specify?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user, during a network partition, exploit the fact that the RequestProperties counter is an AtomicLong that increments on every test() call regardless of whether the request is ultimately processed, causing the limit to be exhausted by rejected requests (those that fail rateLimitBucket or gasLimitBucket checks) and denying service to legitimate requests that would have matched the filter?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the throttle() method iterates over all requestFilter entries in throttleProperties.getRequest() and calls action() for every matching filter, so a request matching multiple filters triggers multiple actions, and during a network partition where filter configuration is replicated inconsistently, cause some nodes to apply double-throttling while others apply none?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: restore(long)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that restore() adds tokens to gasLimitBucket without any upper bound check (bucket4j's addTokens can exceed capacity), and by triggering many failed requests during a partition (each causing restore()), cause the gasLimitBucket to accumulate tokens far beyond its configured capacity, enabling a post-partition burst attack?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user, during a network partition, exploit the fact that the ThrottleManager interface exposes throttle(), throttleOpcodeRequest(), and restore() as separate operations with no transactional guarantee, causing a TOCTOU (time-of-check-time-of-use) race where a request passes throttle() on one thread but the bucket state changes before the EVM executes, allowing more gas consumption than the bucket had tokens for?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the GAS_SCALE_FACTOR of 10,000 means any gas value between 1 and 10,000 is treated as 0 scaled tokens, allowing requests with gas up to 10,000 to bypass gas throttling entirely, and during a network partition where this bypass is exploitable on every node, flood all nodes with 10,000-gas requests that consume no gas bucket tokens?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: throttleOpcodeRequest()] Can an unprivileged external user, during a network partition, exploit the fact that opcodeRateLimitBucket has no synchronization strategy and opcodeRequestsPerSecond defaults to 1, causing concurrent threads on a single partitioned node to race on tryConsume(1) and allow multiple opcode requests per second, violating the strict 1-per-second design limit?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the REJECT action in action() throws ThrottleException('Invalid request') without consuming any per-filter bucket tokens, meaning REJECT-filtered requests still consume a rateLimitBucket token and a gasLimitBucket token before being rejected, and during a partition, use crafted REJECT-matching requests to drain the global rate and gas buckets on each isolated node?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: restore(long)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the asymmetry between throttle() (which consumes from both rateLimitBucket and gasLimitBucket) and restore() (which only restores gasLimitBucket tokens), causing a permanent net depletion of rateLimitBucket tokens during a partition where many requests fail after passing the rate check, eventually exhausting the rate limit and denying service to all users?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user, during a network partition, exploit the fact that the RequestFilter.test() method converts non-String field values (like gas long, estimate boolean) to String via String.valueOf() before applying CONTAINS or EQUALS matching, allowing crafted expression values that match unintended requests (e.g., expression '1' matching gas=1000000 via CONTAINS) and bypassing or triggering filters unexpectedly?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the ThrottleManager is a singleton Spring bean with shared mutable state (the Bucket objects), and during a network partition that causes a node to handle requests from multiple previously-separated client pools simultaneously, cause the shared bucket state to be exhausted faster than the design parameters account for?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: restore(long)] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a scenario where the ContractCallService.restoreGasToBucket() computes gasLimitToRestoreBaseline as (long)(gasLimit * gasLimitRefundPercent / 100f) with floating-point arithmetic, causing rounding errors that systematically over-restore tokens to gasLimitBucket during a partition, gradually inflating the bucket beyond its design capacity?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user, during a network partition, exploit the fact that the rateLimitBucket uses refillGreedy (which refills tokens as fast as possible up to the rate) rather than refillIntervally (which refills at fixed intervals), allowing a burst of up to requestsPerSecond requests at the start of each second on each isolated node, multiplying the burst capacity by the number of partitioned nodes?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the gasLimitBucket is configured with refillGreedy at gasPerSecond tokens per second, and during a network partition where a node is idle (no requests), the bucket fills to full capacity, allowing a post-partition burst of requests that consumes the entire gasPerSecond budget instantly, violating the intended smooth rate limiting?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user, during a network partition, exploit the fact that the ThrottleManager has no circuit-breaker or backpressure mechanism — it either allows or throws ThrottleException — causing a partitioned node to continue accepting requests at full rate even when downstream services (EVM, database) are degraded, leading to resource exhaustion outside the throttle's design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: restore(long)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that restore() is called in a finally block in ContractCallService.doProcessCall(), meaning it is called even when the EVM throws an unexpected RuntimeException (not MirrorEvmTransactionException), causing gas tokens to be restored for requests that may have partially consumed EVM resources, inflating the bucket beyond the intended design?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManager.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: throttle(ContractCallRequest)] Can an unprivileged external user, during a network partition, exploit the fact that the ThrottleManager does not track or limit the number of concurrent in-flight requests (only the rate of new requests), allowing a slow-loris style attack where many requests are accepted within the rate limit but held open simultaneously, exhausting thread pool resources on the isolated node?
